# Literature Review: dexamethasone airway smooth muscle transcriptomics

*8 papers retrieved from PubMed | Generated by Literature Review Agent*

---

## Overview

This structured review synthesizes eight published studies examining the transcriptomic effects of dexamethasone, a potent synthetic glucocorticoid, on airway smooth muscle (ASM) cells in the context of asthma and related airway diseases. The corpus spans publications from 1995 to 2025 and employs a range of transcriptomic platforms, from early oligonucleotide microarrays to contemporary RNA sequencing (RNA-Seq) and chromatin immunoprecipitation sequencing (ChIP-Seq). Collectively, these studies address how glucocorticoid receptor (GR) signaling reshapes gene expression in ASM, with particular emphasis on anti-inflammatory mechanisms, structural remodeling, and the molecular basis of steroid resistance. The body of literature reflects a progressive refinement in both the resolution of transcriptomic interrogation and the mechanistic understanding of glucocorticoid action in this therapeutically critical cell type.

## Key Findings

Across multiple studies, dexamethasone treatment consistently produced broad and reproducible changes in the ASM transcriptome. Himes et al. (2014) identified 316 differentially expressed genes by RNA-Seq in primary human ASM cell lines, including well-established glucocorticoid-responsive genes such as DUSP1, KLF15, PER1, and TSC22D3, as well as less-characterized targets including CRISPLD2. That study further demonstrated that CRISPLD2, which encodes a secreted protein implicated in lung development and endotoxin regulation, modulated the anti-inflammatory response in ASM by suppressing IL-6 and IL-8 expression downstream of IL-1β stimulation. Masuno et al. (2011) extended this landscape by reporting that approximately 7,500 genes exhibited statistically distinguishable changes in expression after 24 hours of dexamethasone treatment in human ASM, with quantitative PCR validation confirming consistency across six independent cell lines. Hakonarson et al. (2001), using high-density oligonucleotide arrays in an earlier study, showed that dexamethasone repressed the majority of IL-1β/TNF-α-induced proinflammatory gene expression—including genes encoding cytokines, chemokines, cellular adhesion molecules, and signal transduction components—while simultaneously enhancing a small subset of transcripts, indicating a bidirectional transcriptional program.

A recurring and functionally significant finding across several studies was the induction of the transcription factor KLF15 by glucocorticoids. Masuno et al. (2011) identified KLF15 as an early GR target in human ASM and demonstrated through in vivo experiments that Klf15-deficient mice exhibited diminished airway hyperresponsiveness following ovalbumin challenge without marked changes in inflammatory cell infiltration, suggesting a direct role for this factor in ASM function. Sasse et al. (2017) built upon this finding by integrating GR ChIP-Seq with transcriptome data from KLF15 overexpression, identifying phospholipase C delta 1 (PLCD1) as a downstream target of KLF15 that represses ASM hypertrophy. That study also identified several additional direct GR targets with putative anti-inflammatory properties, including IRS2, APPL2, RAMP1, and MFGE8, and notably observed GR occupancy at the IL11 and LIF loci in the absence of exogenous ligand, with dexamethasone treatment reducing RNA polymerase II occupancy at these sites, suggesting noncanonical mechanisms of cytokine repression.

Evidence from multiple studies indicated that the transcriptomic response of ASM to dexamethasone was highly context-dependent and could be modulated by co-stimulatory signals. Ouyang et al. (2020) demonstrated through RNA-Seq that the combination of IL-17A and dexamethasone synergistically induced a distinct gene set in human ASM cells, most notably CSF3, which encodes a neutrophil-promoting cytokine. This synergistic induction of CSF3 was mechanistically linked to steroid resistance in type-17 neutrophilic airway disease models, wherein dexamethasone alone failed to alleviate—and in some instances exacerbated—neutrophilic inflammation. Disruption of the IL-17A/dexamethasone synergy through IL-17A inhibition or CSF3 depletion restored dexamethasone sensitivity, underscoring the importance of inflammatory co-stimulatory context in shaping the glucocorticoid transcriptomic response. Similarly, Austin et al. (2017) found that the mRNA expression profile of ASM cells from patients with severe asthma differed significantly from those with nonsevere asthma following dexamethasone exposure, with differential activation of pathways relevant to cellular proliferation and glucocorticoid activity, thereby implicating disease-intrinsic transcriptomic differences in steroid responsiveness.

Long noncoding RNAs (lncRNAs) emerged as an additional regulatory layer in the dexamethasone-responsive ASM transcriptome. Austin et al. (2017) reported significant changes in lncRNA expression following dexamethasone exposure and identified PVT1 as the sole lncRNA with expression levels that distinguished corticosteroid-sensitive nonsevere asthma from corticosteroid-insensitive severe asthma; PVT1 was decreased in the former and elevated in the latter. Knockdown experiments confirmed that PVT1 regulated both proliferation and IL-6 release in severe asthma ASM cells, suggesting a functional role for this lncRNA in the aberrant remodeling phenotype. Beyond the ASM cell context, Pierce et al. (1995) demonstrated in fetal pulmonary artery smooth muscle cells that dexamethasone transcriptionally upregulated tropoelastin expression through increased promoter activity, indicating that glucocorticoid-driven transcriptional programs in smooth muscle may extend to extracellular matrix remodeling during lung development.

## Common Methods

The studies reviewed employed a progressive array of transcriptomic and genomic methods to characterize dexamethasone-induced gene expression changes in ASM. Early investigations, such as that of Hakonarson et al. (2001), relied on high-density oligonucleotide microarrays to survey changes across thousands of transcripts simultaneously, while Austin et al. (2017) utilized a combined mRNA and lncRNA microarray platform to capture both coding and noncoding expression changes. More recent studies transitioned to RNA-Seq, as exemplified by Himes et al. (2014) and Ouyang et al. (2020), enabling unbiased, high-resolution quantification of the entire transcriptome with the ability to detect novel transcripts and apply stringent statistical correction methods such as the Benjamini-Hochberg procedure. Sasse et al. (2017) advanced the field further by integrating transcriptomic data with ChIP-Seq for both the glucocorticoid receptor and RNA polymerase II, enabling genome-wide identification of direct GR binding sites and inference of transcriptional regulatory mechanisms. Quantitative RT-PCR and Western blotting were consistently employed across studies for validation of array or sequencing findings in independent samples and cell lines.

Primary human ASM cell lines derived from donor lung tissue constituted the predominant experimental model across the majority of studies, with treatment protocols typically involving dexamethasone concentrations of 1 µM and incubation periods ranging from 4 to 24 hours. Functional validation strategies included small interfering RNA-mediated knockdown of candidate genes such as CRISPLD2 and PVT1 to assess their roles in cytokine regulation and cell proliferation, respectively. In vivo models were employed in several studies to extend findings beyond cell culture: Masuno et al. (2011) used ovalbumin-sensitized and challenged Klf15-deficient mice to evaluate airway hyperresponsiveness, and Pan et al. (2025) utilized ovalbumin- and ozone-induced murine models of T2-high asthma with neutrophilic inflammation to assess the in vivo efficacy of PLK1 inhibition relative to dexamethasone. Gene ontology and bioinformatic pathway enrichment analyses were widely applied to interpret large-scale transcriptomic datasets in the context of ASM biology and asthma pathophysiology.

## Papers Reviewed

1. Himes BE, Jiang X, Wagner P et al. (2014). RNA-Seq transcriptome profiling identifies CRISPLD2 as a glucocorticoid responsive gene that modulates cytokine function in airway smooth muscle cells.. *PloS one*. PMID: [24926665](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24926665/)

2. Pan Y, Xue Y, Fei X et al. (2025). PLK1 Mediates the Proliferation and Contraction of Airway Smooth Muscle Cells and Has a Role in T2-High Asthma with Neutrophilic Inflammation Model.. *Journal of inflammation research*. PMID: [40162075](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/40162075/)

3. Sasse SK, Kadiyala V, Danhorn T et al. (2017). Glucocorticoid Receptor ChIP-Seq Identifies PLCD1 as a KLF15 Target that Represses Airway Smooth Muscle Hypertrophy.. *American journal of respiratory cell and molecular biology*. PMID: [28375666](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28375666/)

4. Hakonarson H, Halapi E, Whelan R et al. (2001). Association between IL-1beta/TNF-alpha-induced glucocorticoid-sensitive changes in multiple gene expression and altered responsiveness in airway smooth muscle.. *American journal of respiratory cell and molecular biology*. PMID: [11726403](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/11726403/)

5. Ouyang S, Liu C, Xiao J et al. (2020). Targeting IL-17A/glucocorticoid synergy to CSF3 expression in neutrophilic airway diseases.. *JCI insight*. PMID: [32051346](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32051346/)

6. Masuno K, Haldar SM, Jeyaraj D et al. (2011). Expression profiling identifies Klf15 as a glucocorticoid target that regulates airway hyperresponsiveness.. *American journal of respiratory cell and molecular biology*. PMID: [21257922](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21257922/)

7. Pierce RA, Mariencheck WI, Sandefur S et al. (1995). Glucocorticoids upregulate tropoelastin expression during late stages of fetal lung development.. *The American journal of physiology*. PMID: [7900831](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/7900831/)

8. Austin PJ, Tsitsiou E, Boardman C et al. (2017).
